issue.title: 💡 Azure DevOps: Allow filtering by iteration ("Iteration Path") and teams/subteams ("Area Path"), issue type and more (WIQL support?)
issue.body: ### Problem Statement As a followup from https://github.com/super-productivity/super-productivity/pull/6446#discussion_r2798480446 even when testing manually I have the problem to find the actual task/issues to work on. Specifically these filters seem to be useful: * by type ([bug/task/Product backlog items etc.](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/work-items/guidance/scrum-process?view=azure-devops)) * by iteration ("sprint" aka in agile developments you usually work in iterations that are natively supported by ADS) * by area (aka "projects", these may be useful, note ADS has a tree-hierarchy here aka allows for subareas separated by `/`) ### Possible Solution ADS has a "key-value" schema for many properties, as it is very customizable it can have different names, but usually "Iteration Path" is the iteration and "area path" is the "team": Maybe just allow passing a custom filter or so? Actually best supports a custom query langauge: [WIQL reference](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/queries/wiql-syntax?view=azure-devops) It is AFAIK the same and can also [be defined from the GUI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/queries/using-queries?view=azure-devops&tabs=browser). ### Describe alternatives you've considered * As shown above, even in the GUI you can define queries. Maybe allow selecting just "a query" from that list? * Or maybe a simpler implementation by just hardcoding the filter? As the current selection "only assigned to me" etc. allows? ### Additional context IIRC your Jira integration has similar support/also supports their query language, so that may be useful.

outcome.label: merged
outcome.detail: PR #8516 merged 2026-06-22 after a comment-first scoping round (verified live: merged=true)

fidelityNote: Interesting calibration case: the issue body floats 3 mutually-exclusive designs (unclear scope at face value), so analyzeRequirements(body) may legitimately score clearRequirements=false. The eventual merge only happened because John scoped it with a comment before writing code, not from the raw issue alone — a good illustration that 'needs_review' can be the MORE honest verdict than a clean approve/skip binary for this one.
